children.text: A sonic history
"The snares suggest connections to a long history of
pistas

and mixtaps which preceded the breakthrough pop smash and which remain as audible, palpable, if subtle remnants of an unbridled, underground, sample-based past in a genre that has since embraced slick synthesizers and commercial channels" (Marshall, 2009 p. 21).
Origin Narrative
Puerto Rico 1990s
The established narrative situates reggaetón a form of artistic codemixing from Puerto Rico that emerged from the crossroads between Jamaican dancehall, New York hip-hop, and Afro-Panamanian
reggae en español.
The interplay between these genres was not linear, but rather simultaneous.

"In New York, Panamanians, Jamaicans, Puerto Ricans, Dominicans, and African Americans all lived in proximity and were often racialized together. Therefore, genres like reggae and hip-hop resonanted powerfully as "Black musics" which, for all lines still drawn between groups, could embody and express new articulations of community" (Marshall, 2009 p. 33).
reggae en español
meren-rap / merenhouse
bomba and plena
salsa
merengue
(Latin) hip-hop
reggae
Dancehall Reggae & The Dem Bow Beat

The predominant rhythmic orientation of reggaetón is derived directly from dancehall reggae. While there is also influence from roots reggae, dancehall's textures, tempos, rapid-fire flow, and minimalist focus on 3+3+2 cross rhythms demarcated by heavy, synthesized drums have had a stronger influence. One of the most enduring features is the following
riddim
with
a 4/4 pulse of a kick drum and syncopated snares audible in Shabba Ranks' song "Dem Bow" (1990).

children.caption: Skeleton of the Dem Bow Riddim from Marshall (2009)

children.text: "Dancehall and hip-hop have tended to travel together, heard outside their principal sites of production as two sides of the same coin" (Marshall, 2009 p. 25). Dancehall made its way to San Juan not from Kingston, but from New York.

Connected with the growth and influence of New York's Jamaican community in the 1980s, by the 1990s, dancehall reggae had worked its way into the sets of hip-hop DJs and became a culturally charged feature of the New York soundscape. These riddims would later be deeply incorporated into the underground recordings of the early 1990s that laid the musical foundations for reggaeton.
Below are some extremely popular samples that illustrate the prevalence of this riddim throughout the genre's development and global expansion.
Latin Hip-Hop &
Rap en Español

Dancehall's uptake in Panama and Puerto Rico was related to its counter-cultural connotations and close relationship with rap or hip-hop and thus sounds and images of modernity, urbanity, and Blackness. While roots reggae preached pan-African liberation, dancehall embraced more local concerns resonant with hip-hop: crime, drugs, violence, sex, poverty, and corruption.
The proto-reggaetón of the early 1990s drew almost equally on reggae and hip-hop. The artists of this period—
raperos
or
rapeadores
not yet
reggaetoneros
—expressed more of an artistic alignment to hip-hop than reggae, in particular the gangster rap of the 1980s, as a major stylistic influence.

However, the "Latin Rap" or rap in Spanish performed by Puerto Ricans in New York was also a major influence. This subgenre did not diverge stylistically so much as linguistically from hip-hop's sonic template. Puerto Rico's Vico C stands as a foundational figure here, as the artist who first demonstrated that one could rap entirely and compellingly in Spanish (aside from the occasional insertion of hip-hop slang lyrics) and therefore the founder of
rap en español
"I sing hip-hop on top of a reggaetón beat. I don't know how to write it any other way" (Tego Calderón, Marshall 2009 p. 27)
Vico C describes reggaetón as "essentially hip-hop, but with a flavor more compatible to the Caribbean" (Chosen Few Documentary)

Many interviewees in the Chosen Few documentary, for example, distinguish reggaeton from hip hop by noting that the former is more of a baile-centric genre, more appropriate for couple dancing that breaking, where focus is more on the beat than the lyrics. This comes directly from the genre's dancehall baseline and therefore overlap with a great number of Caribbean (dance) genres.
Afro-Panamanian
Reggae en Español

Prior to the reggae's global rise in the 1970s when the genre found popularity outside major sites of Jamaican migration, Afro-Panamanian popular music was heavily based around calypso as performed by local favorites such as Lord Cobra and Lord Panama. By the late 1970s, roots reggae had largely supplanted calypso, largely in the urban contexts of Colón and Panama City. From here, artists such as El General, Nando Boom, and Chicho Man became the earliest proponents of Panamanian reggae, performing some of the first Spanish-language reggae tracks. This direct link between Jamaica and Panama and the embrace of reggae produced a different ecology that than in Puerto Rico, to where the genre arrived indirectly as mediated by New York and hip-hop.

Since the 1980s dancehall reggae has dominanted the scene through the consistent production of Spanish-language cover versions of contemporary reggae hits, typically over replayed versions of the original Jamaican riddims (see below for El General's cover of Shabba Ranks's "Dem Bow"). In this sense, Panamanian reggae is more of an emulation of dancehall, whereas Puerto Rican reggaetón is a reggae-derived music.
These reverent remakes would play a strong role in shaping the dancehall scene in Puerto Rico. The Columbia Records production
Dancehall Reggaespañol

(1991) emphasized the role cover versions played in reggaetón production by pairing "Spanish reggae" with Jamaican recordings. Also such versioning is consistent with the reggae tradition in general and had been in Panama for some time; the use of such riddims in New York was facilitated by producer Karl Miller. The tracks on
Dancehall Reggaespañol
not only attest to a close engagement with contemporary Jamaican reggae, but also bear witness to the crucial role New York played in shaping reggaetón production.
Meren-Rap
These performances found their way to Puerto Rico via DJ Negro, who began operating The Noise nightclub in 1990, and the producer Jorge Oquendo's "meren-rap" project.
Meren-Rap

is an 8-track EP that brings together rising Puerto Rican rap stars and established merengue musicians in a calculated attempt to develop a Latin pop/rap hybrid that could appeal to a wider audience of rap, reggae, merengue, pop, freestyle, and other Latin and urban dance genres (most of which were produced by Vico C). "Te Ves Buena Remix", a sample-based hip-hop inflected atempt at a reggae style track, is perhaps the closest of the project to sound like the productions soon to emerge from the parallel, grassroots development of rap-reggae fusions in San Juan.

This studio experiment is best understood as playing a paradoxically important yet parginal role in the development of reggaetón. The album did make a splash on the Island, but without grassroots support it was quickly supplanted by a similar sort of fusion which, instead of Latin or "tropical" signifiers, tended to foreground the Black, urban, transnational sounds of hip-hop and reggae.
Melaza, Dembow, y el Underground
DJ Playero, DJ Negro, DJ Nelson put the tracks together
Ivy Queen
Daddy Yankee
Baby Rasta and Gringo propel their verses by intoning one of the many familiar melodic contours that Jamaican dancehall DJs have endlessly reworked since the early to mid-1980s
The different names for the genre all had different was of connecting with its Black diasporic roots.
- Calling it melaza (molasses, race as sugar production) directly connects it to cultural politics of Blackness.
- Calling it underground or dembow, or rap and reggae, signaled artistic alignment and a localization of products putatively non-Puerto Rican (rap & reggae).
- Underground calls attention to its marginalized (and proud) status vis-a-vis mainstream Puerto Rican economy, culture, and society.

Even though these terms are derived from hip-hop and reggae, they took on new meaning in Puerto Rico to describe "una fusión tan intensa de rap con reggae que no puede ser clasificada como una cosa o la otra" (Rivera, 1995 fn 50)

Moreover, the term underground referred specifically to how underground recordings literally circulated outside of formal commercial channels, dubbed from cassette to cassette after an initial, small run of master tapes until about 1994. This was in part due to how the music had a reputation as the obscene music of the underclass (e.g., clashes with the National Center on Sexual Exploitation, NCOSE, formerly Morality in Media kept them out of mainstream channels ).
It wasn't until 1999 when DJ Nelson and DJ Coyote managed to get San Juan's mix 107.7 fm to begin a 24/7 reggaetón format

The dembow name refers to the beat from Shabba Rank's production, though it has been abstracted away from its original into a particular rhythmic pattern and a set of specific drum timbres. This became an important signifier that artists could combine with other elements to create distinctiveness within a system of resonant sonic signifiers.

content.caption: Basic Elements of Puerto Rico's localized Dem Bow

children.text: DJ Negro & DJ Playero embodied the process of localizing the foreign-but-familiar through their influential mixtape sets that aided the creation of reggaetón's current style by building off reggae's "riddim method" and hip-hop's sample-based collages. The degree of intertextuality on such recordings is remarkable and charged with significance.

Underground producers employ a primarily sample-based approach according to whatever source seems appropriate, rearticulating both reggae and hip-hop's central practices of reuse and allusion. Marshall (p. 45) describes this as taking reggae's riddim method and hip-hop omnivorous sampling to an unprecedented extreme.
Reggaetón Latino
Luney Tunes
alternates the snares every four measures to create a subtle, stylistically grounded sense of movement against the otherwise repetitive structures of the track

title: A sonic guide to reggaetón's globalization
